what_it_does: Synthesizes a wide codebase read into a catchment document that maps every current place a model is chosen, groups them into five regions, identifies an existing registry/capability resolver foundation, and frames the unification opportunity as routing all selection points through that one mechanism rather than building a new router.
observations: Model selection is scattered across roughly 50 points in 5 regions.
Two 'brains' exist with different jobs: RESIDENT_MODEL (local 4B, cognition engine) and DEFAULT_BRAIN (cloud strong, conversation/RHM layer).
The cognition engine primitives in runtime/cognition.py default to RESIDENT_MODEL and bypass per-role bindings.

A registry foundation already exists: ops/model_capabilities.json, capability_providers(), resolve_role(), resolve_binding(), and resolve_role_binding() which is defined but never called by the engine.
Interactive and clone brains choose models differently; cc_clone.py contains the only context-size-aware router.
Non-chat models (embeddings, rerank, voice/TTS/STT) are mostly already registry-driven or have their own registries.
Many scattered knobs and hardcodes live outside the registry, including config constants, env vars, litellm aliases, and bridge.py line 1064.
Transport to a cloud model is thread-through via openai_transport(base_url), so no new broker or endpoint is needed.
The proposed build is to create one intent-driven entry point, encode extractor/judge tiers in the registry, route bypassing points through it, and absorb scattered defaults.
standouts: The cognition engine bypasses the existing role-based model resolver and pins every primitive to a local 4B.
resolve_role_binding() exists but is never called by the engine.
cc_clone.py has the only context-size-aware model router in the codebase.
Routing cloud models is thread-through transport, not a net-new broker.
conventions: Follows the registry-is-truth principle from AGENTS.md rule 8.
Cites specific file:line locations for claims.
Labels findings provisional and asks readers to verify before building.
Breaks clean separation by maintaining two differently purposed 'brain' constants.
Hardcodes model choices in several places despite the registry.
concerns: Claims are explicitly provisional and require file:line verification before building.
The document mixes descriptive research with prescriptive build implication.
It groups chat, cognition, clones, and embeddings into one 'router' problem, which may oversimplify layer differences.
It flags bridge.py:1064 hardcoding -pro as a conflict without showing the surrounding code.
notes: A research map, not a spec: it catalogs where models are picked today and argues the real work is routing everything through the resolver that already exists.
questions: Which of the cited file:line facts were verified versus inferred from the wide read?
What exact schema will encode tiers like fast/no-think/reasoning/wide in the registry?
How will the proposed single entry point combine intent, role, capability, tier, and context-size constraints?
What is Tim's final stance on the -pro default flagged separately?
How will context-size limits be propagated for non-clone selection points?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches — the file is explicitly a catchment/synthesis of every model-selection point, and it also extends into implementation framing; the extension is signaled, not hidden.
apparent_themes: Route through existing mechanism instead of building anew.
intention_signals: It is trying to turn a scattered set of model-selection decisions into a single intent-aware resolver by exposing and reusing the existing registry/capability foundation.
novelty: It reframes model-routing unification as a catchment problem and argues the router already mostly exists but is bypassed, so the build is gap-closing rather than greenfield.
contribution: A region-by-region map of current model-selection points; the identification of an existing registry/resolver foundation; the distinction between resident and default-brain layers; the context-size-aware clone routing pattern; and the thread-through transport verdict.
summary_for_embedding: A research synthesis mapping roughly 50 model-selection points across five codebase regions, identifying an existing registry/capability resolver foundation and arguing that unification means routing all selection through that existing mechanism.
intention_for_embedding: To provide a verified-before-building catchment of every model-choice site so the team can unify routing around the existing registry resolver rather than create a new router.
